feedback.text: In the digital age, it has become increasingly uncertain of what you actually own and what you will have possession, ownership or even access of. Even if you buy games, that are marketed as something you will have ownership of, it has happened in recent times more than once, that these games get "terminated", as is the case with the game "The Crew" which people bought with their money to possess and be able to play it at the time of purchase and future. These games have certain modes and ways to enjoy it, but due to the publisher/developer it has become increasingly normalised to be connected to the internet at all times, even if the game does not necessarily require it to function and work. Once the servers on the publisher/developer side are turned off, these games become unplayable, due to the constant internet/server requirement. These games have no clear time limit of being a service with any notice of having an ending, which is not consumer-friendly in my opinion. A service must be clearly told to the consumer in a clear way to inform them and not be something, that you pay in the believe of owning said product. Even the return and cancellation of digital games through a store, like the PSN Store, is not friendly and because there are no seemingly unified return/cancellation guidelines/laws in these stores, it has become quite challenging in certain stores to actually get a refund of money in any way or form. Even in cases where games were refunded, like Cyberpunk 2077 or Concord (which was removed from every Console without any prior notice or consent), it was only done by the company not out of necessity, or due to the law, but only due to consumer being frustrated and uncertain, if they would even receive a refund. Even the uncertainty of being able to download your digital purchases is quite a heavy topic for consumers in the forseeable future, with the only clear solution being a physical media like a disc or cartridge. But even then the digital purchases (like DLC, skins, expansions, etc.) might be lost in the future, if there is no clear way to actually keep your owned media in some clear way or form. Some way to ensure consumers have actual ownership of their digital products/media without restrictions through the publisher/developer is needed in this uncertain world and future is definitely needed.
